segments.s655-g1673: همان is a demonstrative ('that' / 'that very') placed before the noun دوری ('distance/avoidance'). دوری از کژی is a noun phrase with از marking source ('avoidance from wrongness'); the coordination و links راه دیو, where راه + دیو is a genitive/attributive construction (in classical Persian the ezāfe is understood: راهِ دیو = 'the way of the demon'). Word order is determiner + noun + prepositional phrase, then coordinated noun phrase.
segments.s655-g1674: بترس is an imperative verb ('fear' / 'be afraid'); it takes a following از‑phrase as its complement. از جهانبان و کیهان خدیو is a coordinated object/phrase introduced by از ('from/of'), listing two titles: جهانبان ('guardian of the world') and کیهان خدیو, where کیهان ('cosmos') modifies خدیو (a title, 'ruler'/'viceroy') in apposition/genitive—typical poetic compounding.
segments.s655-g1675: به فرمان یزدان is an agent/means phrase ('by the command of God'), with به functioning here as 'by' (instrumental). نهاده is a past participle/verb ('has placed, has bestowed') following that phrase, and دو گوش is its direct object ('two ears'). This order (prepositional phrase → verb → object) is normal in classical Persian.
segments.s655-g1676: وزیشان is the noun وز ('blast, gust') plus the enclitic شان ('their'), functioning adverbially/locatively ('by/through their blast' or 'because of them'). نباشد is a negative subjunctive/existential form ('let there not be' / 'there is not'), followed by کسی ('anyone/no one'—here negated, so 'no one'). با خروش is a prepositional phrase ('with a cry/uproar') modifying the predicate. The inversion 'نباشد کسی' is a typical classical negative existence construction.
segments.s655-g1677: ازان پس is an adverbial phrase ('after that' / 'thereupon') with ازان ('from that/thereupon') + پس ('after/then'). بپرسیدش is an imperative verb form ('ask him') where the enclitic -ش = 'him/her/it' is attached to the verb. از پادشا is a source phrase ('of/from the king') introduced by از; verb + object/order is normal here.
segments.s655-g1678: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause ('who/for he'), linking back to 'the king'. فرماروانست is an archaic/political predicate meaning 'is sovereign / rules' (a single lexical unit). بر پارسا uses the preposition بر ('over, upon') with پارسا ('the pious') as its object — 'over the pious.' The clause gives the king's qualifying quality.
segments.s655-g1679: کز ایشان is an archaic interrogative/prepositional phrase ('which of them' / 'from among them'), with کز functioning like 'which of' and ایشان 'them'. کدامست is the fused question word + copula ('which is it?'). پیروزبخت is a nominal predicate ('fortunate one') placed after the question form — overall asking 'which among them is the fortunate one.'
segments.s655-g1680: که introduces a relative clause modifying 'پیروزبخت' ('who/that'). باشد is a subjunctive/present form of 'to be' here functioning as the copula ('is' / 'may be'); به گیتی is a prepositional phrase ('in the world') with به used to mark realm/place. سزاوار تخت is a predicate phrase ('worthy/deserving of the throne'), with تخت ('throne') as the object of سزاوار (the ezafe relationship is implied in poetic prose).
segments.s655-g849: مگر here functions as the exception marker 'except'. دخت خاتون is a genitival noun phrase 'the daughter of the khatun' (noun + possessor). که introduces a relative clause 'who…' and افسر نداشت is a past negative predicate: افسر 'helmet' + نداشت ('did not have'), with the verb at the end as typical in Persian.
segments.s655-g850: همان is a demonstrative/emphatic 'that very' or 'she herself'. یاره here means 'beloved/companion' (a poetic reference to the woman). وطوق و گوهر نداشت is coordinated object + negative verb: 'she had no necklace and no jewel' (و links the two objects; ندداشت is the same past negative verb carried over).
segments.s655-g851: یکی is an indefinite 'one / a certain (woman)'. جامه کهنه is a noun + postnominal adjective 'an old garment'. بد here functions adverbially 'poorly/ill' modifying the following locative expression; بر is the preposition 'on'. The final token برش in the segmented input does not correspond cleanly to the listed senses (it likely represents a cliticized form meaning 'on her'), so the verbless clause describes appearance rather than asserting an action.
segments.s655-g852: کلاهی is a noun 'a hat' (the -ی can be a nominative/indefinite form). زمشک names the material/ornament (here a type of fur or material; in the supplied senses it appears as 'barberry' but the phrase functions as a material/ornament name). ایزدی is an adjective 'divine' or 'sacred' modifying زمشک, and بر سرش is the prepositional phrase 'upon her head' (بر 'on' + سر 'head' + ش enclitic 'her').
segments.s655-g853: This is a negative existential describing the face: ز + کرده functions as 'made/worked' (literally 'of what was done'), به رخ 'on the face' (به + رخ), بر نگارش نبود = 'there was no painting/inscription' (بر + نگارش + نبود). The clause means that no artificial decoration or writing was on her face.
segments.s655-g854: جز ... نبود is the formula 'there was nothing except ...'. آرایش is 'adornment/ornament', کردگارش is 'her Creator' (کردگار 'Creator' + ش enclitic 'her'), and نبود is the past existential 'there was not / there was only'. Together: 'there was nothing except the Creator's adornment.'
segments.s655-g855: یکی سرو بود (بد here is a poetic spelling of بود 'was') gives a metaphor: 'one was a cypress' (سرو = cypress, used for a tall graceful person). بر سرش ماه نو is a locative phrase 'on her head (was) the new moon' — the line stacks two pictorial images (cypress and new moon) with the copula implied/expressed by بد(بود).
segments.s655-g856: فروزان is a predicative adjective 'radiant' or 'shining'. ز here means 'from' or 'because of', so ز دیدار او 'from seeing her' indicates cause: the moon (or radiance) grew brighter at the sight of her. گاه نو 'a new moment / newly' is an adverbial phrase reinforcing the freshness of the moon; overall: 'the new moon shone, radiant at the sight of her.'
segments.s655-g465: سخن هرچ گفتند اندر نهان is built as 'noun + modifier + verb + adverbial'. سخن (noun) is 'words/speech' and هرچ ('whatever') qualifies it; گفتند is 3rd-person plural past 'they said' (narrative). اندر نهان is an adverbial phrase 'in secret/in private' placed after the verb — typical Classical Persian word-order flexibility.
segments.s655-g466: بگفتند با شهریار جهان: بگفتند here functions as 'they told/reported' (past, 3rd-plural); با introduces the person addressed/affected (classical use where با can mark the addressee), so با شهریار جهان = 'to the king of the world' (شهریار جهان is a title, noun + genitive modifier). The clause is a simple transitive statement with the indirect object following the verb.
segments.s655-g467: به گنجور فرمود پس شهریار contains a classical ordering: فرمود (past honorific 'he commanded/said') takes an indirect object marked by به (here 'to/for the treasurer' — به گنجور). پس ('then') and شهریار ('the king') follow; شهریار is the subject placed after the verb (poetic inversion).
segments.s655-g468: که آرد به دشت آلت کارزار: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that' / relative/purpose clause). آرد is an archaic/poetic present form (cf. آورد) meaning 'bring(s)' or 'should bring' and appears here as the verb of the subordinate clause. به دشت is the goal 'into/to the plain', and آلت کارزار is a genitive NP 'the implements/instruments of battle' (آلت = instrument, کارزار = battle).
segments.s655-g469: بیاورد خفتان وخود و زره: بیاورد is the jussive/optative 3rd-singular ('let him bring' / 'that he bring') used in subordinate commands. It introduces a list of objects to be brought: خفتان (here an object/item/person — lexeme uncertain in this context), وخود 'and himself', و زره 'and (the) armor'. The conjunction و links the items; order shows objects listed after the verb.
segments.s655-g470: بفرمود تا برگشاید گره: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past ('he commanded/ordered'). تا here marks purpose/result ('so that' / 'in order that'). برگشاید is a subjunctive/potential verb form 'may open / might be opened' (present-subjunctive flavor in epic diction). گره 'knot' is the direct object placed at the end in typical Persian order.
segments.s655-g471: گشاده برون کرد زورآزمای: this phrase places the participial/adjectival گشاده ('opened') before the compound verb برون کرد ('took/brought out'), a common epic sequence where an adjectival state precedes the verb. زورآزمای ('strength-tester' / 'strongman') is the agent/epithet and follows the verb (postverbal subject), a stylistic inversion found in classical Persian.
segments.s655-g472: نبرداشتی جوشن او زجای is an archaic/poetic sequence where نبرداشتی appears as a negative or conditional verbal form ('would not have taken' / 'did not take' in sense) followed by the object جوشن او ('his cuirass/armor') and the prepositional/adverbial زجای ('from its place'). Word order here is verb–object–prepositional phrase; the verb form is irregular/archaic and conveys inability/negation in context.
segments.s655-g457: هنر جوید is a classical present/persistent verbal clause: هنر (the object/title 'skill' or 'art') precedes the verb جوید (classical present 'he seeks' — bare present without modern prefix). وهیچ is a negative/adversative particle here meaning 'and not at all' (an emphatic negation), which scopes over the following verb phrase; پیچد عنان is verb + object (پیچد 'twists/turns/pulls' + عنان 'the reins'), so the whole segment reads 'he seeks skill, and does not at all pull the reins.'
segments.s655-g458: به کردار پیکر نماید سنان is a prepositional/manner phrase + verb + subject in a poetic order: به کردار ('in the manner/by the deed') modifies نماید ('shows' — classical 3rd‑person present/past form used here as 'shows'/'display[s]'), and سنان (the spear) appears after the verb as the subject (verb–subject inversion is common in poetry). پیکر here means 'figure/form' and the phrase means roughly 'the spear shows a figure by its action.'
segments.s655-g459: هنر‌گر is a nominal title ('the artisan/artist/adept') used as the subject or vocative; نمودی is the classical past 2nd‑person form 'you showed' (in epic style the verb form may address or recount action), and به ما is the indirect object 'to us'. شهریار is an address/appellation 'O king' in apposition or vocative position. The clause functions as 'You (or the artisan) showed art to us, O king.'
segments.s655-g460: ازو داشتی هر یکی یادگار is a straightforward past possession clause: ازو 'from him' is the source, داشتی here functions as past 'had' agreeing with the indefinite subject, هر یکی 'each one' is the subject/quantifier, and یادگار is the possessed noun 'memento/keepsake'. Word order (source — verb — subject — object) reflects poetic flexibility but reads 'each one had a keepsake from him.'
segments.s655-g461: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; هریک 'each one' is the subject; برفتی is a classical past form meaning 'went away/left' (prefix بر‑ adds the sense 'off/away'); برشاه خویش is a prepositional phrase 'to/upon his own king' (برشاه = 'to the king', خوی + ش = 'his own' in bound form). The clause therefore reads 'when each one departed to his own king.'
segments.s655-g462: سخن داشتی is an idiomatic light‑verb construction 'to have speech / to speak' with داشتی as past 'had' (the verb) and سخن 'a word / conversation' as its object; یارهمراه خویش ('his accompanying friend / his companion') is the person with whom the speech is held. Note that Persian often omits an explicit preposition 'با' ('with') in poetry; so the phrase means 'he had a word with his companion.'
segments.s655-g463: بگفتی is the past/perfective 'you said' (classical 2nd‑person past form); که introduces the subordinate clause 'that'; چون in this context is temporal 'when'; شاه is the noun 'king' and نوشین‌روان is an epithet 'sweet‑souled' modifying شاه. The fragment begins 'You said that when the sweet‑souled king ...'.
segments.s655-g464: بدیده نبینند پیر و جوان is a negated present/potential clause: نبینند is the classical present/subjunctive 'they do not see' (negative), and پیر و جوان 'old and young' are the persons referenced. بدیده here functions as a participial/adverbial element relating to sight (literally 'seen/with the eye') — the compound sense is poetic and not identical to the lemma sense 'ill‑fated' offered elsewhere, so none of the provided lemma senses fits exactly in this context.
segments.s655-g377: This small clause is a simple past assertion: 'یکی' (a certain one) + object 'نامه' (a letter) + the archaic past verb 'بنبشت' (he wrote). The prepositional phrase 'با آفرین' follows to mark the manner/incipit of the letter — literally ‘with “Afarin”’ (i.e. beginning the letter with that word or with praise).
segments.s655-g378: 'سخندان' is an adjective/noun (the eloquent one) qualifying the writer or the style. The sequence 'چینی چو ار تنگ چین' is a simile: 'چینی' (here ‘china/porcelain’) + comparative particle 'چو' (as/like) + conditional/particle 'ار' (if/supposing) + modifier 'تنگ چین' (literally ‘narrow‑fold’). Grammatically, the chain compares the letter’s eloquence to the delicate, tightly folded (or finely‑pleated) quality associated with Chinese porcelain; 'ار' is poetic/conditional flavoring within the simile.
segments.s655-g379: The verb 'برفت' here functions as an archaic past of رفتن ('they went/rode off'); the phrase that follows 'آن خرد یافته ده سوار' is a noun phrase meaning 'those ten who had acquired wisdom' or 'those ten wise riders'. Word order places the demonstrative 'آن' and the adjectival phrase 'خرد یافته' before the numeral+noun 'ده سوار' (a poetic order to emphasize the qualities of the riders).
segments.s655-g380: 'نهان' is an adverbial/manner modifier meaning ‘secretly’; it qualifies the proceeding action (their departure/speech). 'پرسخن' (literally ‘full of speech’ / talkative) specifies that they were conversing. 'تا' here marks the endpoint/duration ('until'), and 'درشهریار' is a locative expression meaning 'into/at the presence of the king' — together meaning they spoke secretly up to the king’s presence.
segments.s655-g381: This temporal clause begins with the indirect object 'به کسری' ('to Khosrow'). 'چو' here is temporal ('when'), and the verb 'برداشتند' is past 3rd‑plural meaning 'they carried/brought' — its object is 'آگهی' ('news/information'). So the clause reads 'When they brought news to Khosrow...'.
segments.s655-g382: This main clause has an implied subject (Khosrow from the previous clause) performing the verb 'بیاراست' (past, 'he adorned' or 'caused to be ornately prepared'). The object is 'ایوان شاهنشهی' ('the imperial hall/portico'); the adjective 'شاهنشهی' modifies 'ایوان' to mark it as imperial. The verb is simple past, describing the king’s action on hearing the news.
segments.s655-g383: 'بفرمود' is the courtly/honorific past of گفتن/فرمودن meaning 'he commanded/ordered/said'. It introduces the subordinate clause with 'تا' used purposively/introducing the commanded action ('that/so that they...'). 'پرده برداشتند' is the subordinate clause in past 3rd‑plural ('they removed/lifted the curtain'), completing the command.
segments.s655-g384: 'ز' (poetic 'from') marks source/location; 'درگاهشان' ('his/their court/presence' — the pronominal suffix is an honorific reference to the king) names the place. 'شاد' is an adverb/adjectival manner word ('glad/joyful') modifying the verb 'بگذاشتند' (past 3rd‑plural, 'they let go/dismissed' or 'they set (them) free/allowed (them) to depart'). Read together: from his presence they were joyfully dismissed — i.e., the king had them dismissed with favor.
segments.s655-g49: The clause opens with the conditional particle اگر ('if'). شاه ایران and خاقان چین are noun + country appositions meaning 'the Shah of Iran' and 'the Khaghan of China' (classical/poetic style often omits the ezâfe). و simply joins the two rulers.
segments.s655-g50: بسازند is 3rd-person plural present/subjunctive 'they make' or 'they reconcile' after the conditional اگر. وز دل is poetic-contracted spelling (و + ز / و + از) meaning 'and from the heart'; وز here functions like 'and from'. کنند is the plural present 'they do' used as a light verb, and آفرین is an exclamation/noun 'praise' (they 'say "afarin"'). Word order is slightly inverted (verb کننـد before the noun آفرین), typical of older/poetic Persian.
segments.s655-g51: هراسست is a past tense verb 'he feared' or 'he was seized with dread'. زین دوستی is an ablative/causal phrase 'from/ because of this friendship' (زین = poetic/archaic form corresponding to 'از این'); بهر ما = 'for our sake' ('bahr' marking beneficiary).
segments.s655-g52: برین is the locative 'بر + این' ('on/at that'), here 'on that occasion' or 'in that case'; روی = 'face' / 'occasion' in metaphorical sense. The predicate is ویران شود ('may become ruined' — شود is subjunctive/auxiliary used for potential/future). The subject شهرما ('our city') comes after the verb—classical inversion is common in poetic lines.
segments.s655-g53: بباید is the modal impersonal 'it must / one ought to'. یکی qualifies the action as 'one / a single' or 'someone' depending on context; تاختن is the verbal noun 'to charge/ride at' and ساختن here is a light verb 'to make/perform' combined with تاختن to mean 'to carry out a charge'. The sequence shows infinitival/verbal-noun constructions common in older Persian.
segments.s655-g54: This segment gives purpose/target: جهان 'the world' (here adverbial 'out into the world' or 'abroad'), از + فرستاده marks the target/agent 'against the envoy', and پرداختن is an older verb 'to set upon / to attend to / to deal with' (here 'to deal with' or 'make an attack on'). Together they express 'to send someone out into the world to deal with the envoy.'
segments.s655-g55: زلشکر appears as a proper name or title (uncertain/obscure in the lexicon) and is the subject doing the choosing. یکی نامور is an object phrase 'a certain/famed one' with the adjective after the numeral, and برگزید is simple past 'he chose/selected.'
segments.s655-g56: سرافراز جنگی is a nominal phrase 'a proud/triumphant warrior' (سرافراز qualifying جنگی). چنانچون is a comparative 'just as / as', and سزید is an older/past form meaning 'it befitted / he deserved' (passive/fitting sense): 'as was fitting/deserved.' The clause therefore praises the fit outcome.
segments.s655-g353: ز + noun (شاهان) here means 'among/from the kings' (poetic). The verb نجوید (present) follows the topical phrase and takes the indefinite کسی as subject; with the following exception clause (مگر...) the whole clause is understood negatively: 'no one (کسی ... مگر...) seeks'. جای اوی is a possessive phrase: جای 'place/position' followed by the archaic possessive اوی ('his').
segments.s655-g354: مگر introduces the exception ('unless' / 'except that'). تیره is an adjectival predicate meaning 'dark/ominous' and باشد is the subjunctive/present form of بودن used here to state the condition. دل و رای اوی is a coordinated noun phrase ('his heart and his counsel'), with اوی as the poetic possessive 'his'.
segments.s655-g355: که begins an explanatory/reason clause ('for/that'), and با فر او literally 'with his far' (فر = divine royal glory). The construction تخت را شاه نیست uses را to mark تخت as the definite object/topic and شاه نیست functions as 'there is no king for the throne' or idiomatically 'the throne is not (a proper) throne without him' — a poetic word order where predicate follows the marked noun.
segments.s655-g356: بدیدار او is a poetic expression meaning 'in his appearance / to be seen as him' (بدیدار = 'in sight/appearance'); در فلک sets the location 'in the heavens/sky' and ماه نیست states the negative 'there is no moon'. The sense is a conventional hyperbole: no moon in heaven matches his visage.
segments.s655-g357: همی is a continuous/adverbial particle meaning 'constantly/always' here. باژ (a cry/clang) with خواهد (future auxiliary) forms 'will raise a cry' i.e. 'his fame will be sounded/declared'. The sequence ز هند وز روم is poetic for 'from Hind and from Rum' — وز here is actually a poetic contraction of 'و ز' ('and from'), so read as two sources.
segments.s655-g358: ز جایی که is 'from a/place where...' (ز = from; جایی که = where). گنجست is a concise predicative form meaning broadly 'is treasure/contains treasure' (poetic laconic form), and آباد بوم is a noun phrase 'prosperous/inhabited homeland'. Together they specify the kinds of places from which his renown spreads.
segments.s655-g359: خداوند here functions as 'the lord/sovereign' (an honorific), and تاجست is the contracted poetic form 'تاج است' ('is a crown'). The conjunction و links the next nominal phrase; زیبای تخت is an adjectival/genitive phrase 'the beauty/ornament of the throne' (زیبا + possessive relation to تخت). The syntax is ornamental listing of attributes.
segments.s655-g360: This segment lists further epithets in coordination: جهاندار 'world‑holder / sovereign', و بیدار 'and awake/alert' (literal 'awake' as an epithet), و پیروز بخت is a compound where پیروز 'victorious' is qualified by بخت 'fortune/fate' (i.e. 'victorious by fortune' or 'fortunate victor'). The string is appositive to the sovereign named earlier.
segments.s655-g281: "هرآنگه که" is a temporal conjunction meaning "whenever/whenver that"; here it introduces a subordinate temporal clause. "فرمان دهد" is a 3rd-person singular present/past-classical form (literary) meaning "gives (the) command"; the verb precedes its verbal object "کارزار" for poetic word-order (literal: "gives command battle").
segments.s655-g282: نبیند is a negative subjunctive/optative form (literary) meaning "may (he) not see" or "let be not seen"; the clause is a jussive wish. ز ما = "from us" (poetic "ز" = "from"); کاهلی = "laziness/negligence" is the thing not to be seen; شهریار stands at the end as the addressee/subject "the king/sovereign" (poetic emphasis by placing the subject after the verb and object).
segments.s655-g283: ازان پس = "after that/thereupon" (ازان = "thereupon" + پس = "then/after"). چو بنشست is a temporal clause "when he sat down" (چو = "when"); بنشست is the classical past form "he sat (down)". با رای‌زن = "with (his) counselors/advisers" (با = "with"); رای‌زن is a counsellor/one who gives counsel.
segments.s655-g284: بزرگان وکسری = "the nobles and Khosrow/the king" (note the conjunction و is attached here to کسری in the manuscript form). شدند is the past plural of شدن "became/turned into" and in this idiom means "gathered" or "were made"; انجمن is the noun "assembly/meeting" that they constituted — word order in poetry places predicate elements after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s655-g285: همی‌بود is an iterative/imperfect literary form "it continued to be/was". ازین گونه = "in this manner/way" (ازین = "of this" or "from this", گونه = "kind/manner"). تا introduces the temporal limit "until"; ماه نو = "the new moon/new month" (poetic calendar reference).
segments.s655-g286: برآمد is a past tense verb "arose/appeared". نشست here functions as a noun "a sitting/session/assembly" (not the verb "to sit"); thus "a session arose" or "a meeting was held". از برگاه نو = "from the new court/royal platform" (از = "from", برگاه = "royal court/platform", نو = "new"). The phrase shows poetic inversion (verb before noun).
segments.s655-g287: تو گفتی که = "you said that"; گفتی is the 2nd-person past tense "you said" (classical narrative). جامی ز یاقوت زرد = "a goblet/cup of yellow ruby"; ز indicates material/composition "of/from" (a cup made of ruby or set with ruby), and زرد here qualifies the ruby as "yellow" (poetic color phrase).
segments.s655-g288: نهادند is the 3rd-person plural past of نهادن "they placed/they set" (they = agents implied). بر = "on/upon"; چادر = "tent/veil"; لاژورد follows the noun as an adjective ("lapis/azure") describing the tent ("lapis-colored tent" or "lapis lazuli tent"). The whole means "they placed (it) upon the lapis tent."
segments.s655-g1761: دگر is an adverb placed at the clause head meaning 'again / moreover' (here: 'another, additionally'). آنک is a relative pronoun 'he who / that which'. دارد is the present/present-habitual verb 'has/possesses'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from', marking the source (ز یزدان = 'from God'). سپاس is the object 'praise/thanks' — together 'دارد ز یزدان سپاس' = 'has praise from God'.
segments.s655-g1762: بود is the past copula 'was' introducing the predicate. دانشی is a genitive/nominal adjective 'of learning / learned' (literally 'a man of knowledge'). مرد is the head noun 'man/person'. نیکی‌شناس is a compound adjective/noun 'one who knows or recognizes goodness' (i.e. a good-discerning or virtuous man) in apposition to مرد.
segments.s655-g1763: بدو is the prepositional clitic 'to him' (introducing the addressee). گفت here functions as the speech verb 'said' (note: the supplied sense list does not include a simple 'say' entry). کسری is the subject 'Khosrow' placed next to the verb (word order is flexible). که is the complementizer introducing the reported question/utterance. کرده is the past participle 'done' used with چه to ask 'what have (you) done'. چه is the interrogative 'what'. به is the preposition 'to/toward' preparing the object of the action.
segments.s655-g1764: چه is another interrogative 'what'. ناکرده is the negative participle 'not done / left undone' (so together with the previous clause it contrasts 'what you have done / not done'). از is the preposition 'from / toward / with regard to' and here introduces the affected party. شاه is 'the king'. وز is a poetic fused form (و + ز) meaning 'and (from)' — i.e. 'and from / and toward'; it links a second beneficiary/affected party. مرد is 'man / person'. مه is an epithet 'great, mighty' (used adjectivally here: 'the mighty man').
segments.s655-g1765: چه بهتر is a fixed exclamatory phrase 'how much better / far better'. کزو is a relative/adverbial 'from which / from whom' (here linking to the following noun). باز is the noun 'falcon' (a bird of prey) rather than the adverb 'again'. داریم is the present 'we have / we keep'. چنگ here is used in the sense 'claw, talon' or 'that which is seized by the talon' (so together implying 'a falcon that has possession/seized prey').
segments.s655-g1766: گرفته is the perfect/past participle 'having seized / taken' (it qualifies the preceding notion of the falcon). چه بهتر repeats 'how much better'. ز is the poetic comparative preposition 'than' here. بهر is 'for the sake of / on account of'. درنگ is 'delay / tarrying' — the clause says that possessing a seized falcon is far preferable to delay.
segments.s655-g1767: چه بهتر again 'how much better'. ز is the comparative preposition 'than'. فرمودن here carries the courtly sense 'to command' (not merely 'to say'). و links the two infinitives. داشتن is the infinitive 'to have / to possess' — the phrase contrasts commanding/possessing with the alternative in the next segment.
segments.s655-g1768: وگر is the conditional 'but if / if indeed'. مرد is 'man' (the person in question). را is the definite object/topic marker highlighting the man as the patient. خوار is the adjective 'despised / lowly'. بگذاشتن is the verb 'to leave / abandon' (here 'to leave someone in a despised state'): together 'مرد را خوار بگذاشتن' = 'to leave a man despised'.
segments.s655-g889: خردمند is the noun (subject) placed before the verb for emphasis. بنشست is a past tense (3rd‑person singular) verb meaning 'sat down' or 'took a seat'. با introduces accompaniment ('with') and رای‌زن is an agent noun in the oblique introduced by با ('with a counselor' or 'with counselors'); word order is Subject + Verb + prepositional (accompanying) phrase.
segments.s655-g890: بپالود is a past transitive verb (3rd‑person) here meaning 'refined/arranged/cleansed' and continues the action after the previous clause. 'زایوان شاه انجمن' is a compact genitive chain typical of poetic Persian (no ezāfe written) — nouns are stacked to mean something like 'the zayvān of the king of the assembly' or more generally 'the royal hall/portion of the assembly'; the whole phrase functions as the object of بپالود.
segments.s655-g891: چو is a subordinating conjunction here meaning 'when'. پردخته is a past participle/adjective 'well‑wrought/finished' modifying جایگاه. شد is the past auxiliary 'became/was (made)', so the clause reads 'when the place was finished'. نشست is used as an intransitive verb 'sat/took seats' (agreement with an implied plural subject), completing the clause.
segments.s655-g892: برفتند is past tense (3rd‑person plural) 'they went' or 'they proceeded'. با introduces the accompaniment/instrument 'with', and زیج رومی is a noun phrase 'Roman/Byzantine zij' (zij = astronomical tables or handbook). بدست here functions adverbially as 'in (their) hand' or 'with ... in hand' rather than the simple prepositional sense.
segments.s655-g893: ستاره شناسان is a compound where ستاره ('star') is modified by شناسان (an agentive plural 'those who know/make known'), i.e. 'astronomers' or 'star‑experts'. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. کندآوران is another plural agentive noun in apposition; in poetic diction such compound occupational names are juxtaposed without further connectors.
segments.s655-g894: هرآنکس که is a relative/indefinite phrase 'whoever/whomever (it was) that...'. بودند is past (3rd‑person plural) 'they were'. ز ایشان is the poetic genitive 'of them' and سران is the predicate noun 'chiefs/leaders'. The clause therefore states that those named were the leaders among them; the copula is expressed by بودند.
segments.s655-g895: بفرمود is an honorific/past reporting verb 'he commanded/said'. تا introduces a purpose/result clause 'that/so that'. هر کرا is an archaic indefinite 'whoever', and بود is the past copula 'had/was' (here 'had' in the sense 'possessed'). مهر in this context is best read as 'seal' or 'official token' (i.e., someone bearing the royal seal or commission) and is the predicate of the subordinate clause.
segments.s655-g896: بجستند is past tense (3rd‑person plural) 'they sought/sought out'. یک سر is 'one head' where سر functions as 'chief/leader' (here the direct object). شمار سپهر is a descriptive genitive 'the counter/enumerator of the heavens' (شمار = counting/tally, سپهر = sky/heavens), so the clause means 'they sought one chief — the astronomer who counts the heavens'. Poetic Persian frequently stacks nouns without an explicit ezāfe.
segments.s655-g753: کسی راکه is the object phrase with the enclitic relative 'که' attached to را (literally 'the person whom'); it means 'the person whom/whomever'. خواهد کند is a 3rd-person verbal sequence where خواهد (future/willing) combines with کند (do/make) — a classical/poetic periphrasis meaning 'wills/chooses to make'. ارجمند is a predicative adjective acting as the object-complement 'esteemed/honored' (i.e. 'make X esteemed').
segments.s655-g754: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; پستی is the noun 'lowliness/low status' serving as the source term. برآرد is 3rd-person present/subjunctive-poetic 'he raises/brings up' (بر + آرد), and به چرخ بلند is a prepositional goal 'to the high charkh' where چرخ here means 'sky/heaven' or a lofty station and بلند 'lofty/high' qualifies it.
segments.s655-g755: دگر here means 'another/others' (someone else) and مانده is the participial/past-stative 'remains/is left'. اندر is the older preposition 'in', and بد روزگار is a compound meaning 'bad days/ill-fortune' (بد qualifying روزگار). The whole phrase = 'others are left in bad times.'
segments.s655-g756: چو is the temporal/concessive 'when/if' introducing the clause. نیکی is 'goodness/benefit' and نخواهد is the negative future/volitive 'will not' (3rd person). بدو is the clitic combination 'به + او' meaning 'to him/them', and کردگار is the agent 'the Creator/God' placed at the end in poetic word order. Literally: 'when the Creator will not (give) goodness to him.'
segments.s655-g757: بهرنیکی is a fused prepositional/nominal phrase 'for the sake of goodness' or 'for any good'; از وی = 'from him' (از + وی). شناسم is 1st-person present 'I know/recognize' used here idiomatically 'I acknowledge' or 'I give' (i.e. express) سپاس ('thanks'). Word order places the object 'سپاس' after the verb, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s655-g758: وگر is the conditional 'but if'. بد is a noun 'evil/bad (deed or event)'. کنم is 1st-person present/subjunctive 'I do' (here used to form a hypothetical 'if [evil] I should do/if evil comes'). ز + و together represent 'from him' (ز = 'from', و here stands for the old clitic pronoun), and دل اندر هراس is the predicative phrase 'the heart in fear' meaning 'I am afraid' (دل = heart, اندر = in, هراس = fear). Note: the clitic pronoun is written separately in the gloss, so the listed sense for the token و does not match a conjunction and thus is not covered by the provided senses.
segments.s655-g759: نباید is an impersonal negative modal 'it must not / there ought not to'. که introduces the subordinate clause 'that ...'. جان is 'life/soul' as the grammatical subject, باشد is the 3rd-person present/subjunctive ('be' — here 'there should be'), اندر = 'in', and تنم is 'my body' (تن + 1st-person enclitic). The clause literally: 'There ought not to be a life in my body.'
segments.s655-g760: اگر = 'if'. بیم و امید = 'fear and hope' (بیم ... و ... امید joined by the conjunction و). از + و again stands for 'from him' (از = 'from'; the following و is a clitic representing 'him' rather than the conjunction), and برکنم is 1st-person present/subjunctive 'I remove/dismiss' (i.e. 'if I cast aside'). The whole conditional: 'if I cast away fear and hope of him.' Note: the second token و in this segment is the pronominal clitic 'him', which is not one of the conjunction senses provided, so its sense index is -1.
segments.s655-g609: پس here is a discourse connective 'moreover/then'. پرده ما is a possessive noun phrase 'our veil/curtain' (پرده + enclitic/possessive ما = 'our curtain'). بسی is an adverbial quantifier 'very/many' (poetic use) qualifying the following noun phrase. دخترست is an archaic/poetic predicate: دختر + -ست (old form of 'است') giving the sense 'there are maidens / is maidens' (the copula -ست stands for 'is/are').
segments.s655-g610: که introduces a relative clause 'who/which'. برتارک is a fused prepositional phrase (بر تارک) meaning 'on the brow / on the crown of the head'. بانوان is the genitive 'of the ladies/women'. افسرست is again noun + archaic copula '-ست' meaning 'is a diadem/helmet' (افسر used poetically for a head‑ornament/crown). The clause modifies 'دختر' from the previous segment.
segments.s655-g611: یکی را is an indefinite object phrase marked by the object marker را ('one/some person' as the object). به نام شهنشه کنیم is a causative/nominal construction: به نام + title = 'under the name/title of'; شهنشه is the title 'Shahanshah'; کنیم is first-person-plural present/subjunctive/hortative 'let us make / let us call'. Together: 'let us make/call someone Shahanshah.'
segments.s655-g612: ز کار وی is a prepositional phrase 'concerning/from his deed(s)/affair' (ز here = 'about / concerning'); اندیشه کوته کنیم is 'let us shorten our thought/consideration' where اندیشه = 'consideration/thought' and کوته (adjective used adverbially) = 'short/brief'; کنيم again hortative 'let us do' so the clause means 'let us be brief about his deeds.'
segments.s655-g613: چو here functions as a conditional conjunction 'if/when'. پیوند سازیم is 'we form a tie/bond' (پیوند = bond; سازیم = 1pl subjunctive of ساختن 'to make/form'). با او = 'with him'. به خون = 'by/with blood' i.e. 'by blood (a kinship/blood‑tie)'. The whole clause: 'If we bind ourselves to him by blood...'.
segments.s655-g614: نباشد here expresses negative existence ('there will not be' / 'there is not'); کس in negative context = 'anyone / no one'. اورا is the object pronoun 'him' (older object form). به بد رهنمون = 'to a bad guide' / 'to lead astray' (به بد = toward evil/badly; رهنمون = guide). The clause thus states that 'there will be no one to misguide/lead him badly.'
segments.s655-g615: بدو = 'to him / for him' (prefixed prepositional pronoun). نازش is ناز + the possessive -ش = 'his affection/favor' (i.e. 'affection shown to him'); وسرفرازی = و + سرفرازی 'and exaltation/pride/honour'. بود is the past/imperf. copula 'was/will be' used here to state a quality or consequence ('he had / he was granted').
segments.s655-g616: وزو is a contracted poetic form meaning 'from him / with him' (in the supplied senses list the expected sense is not given, so index marked unknown). بگذری (بگذرد in sense) = 'passes / proceeds' (here in narrative sense 'will come/attend'). جنگ و بازی = 'war and play' i.e. 'battle and sport/tournaments.' بود again is the copula 'there was / there would be'. Overall: 'He will receive favour and honour, and war and sport will be associated with him.'
segments.s655-g1201: ابا is a poetic form functioning like با "with" and introduces the list of items. The words that follow are a coordinated list (هدیه و جامه و سیم و زر) — nouns joined by و "and"; the whole segment names the gifts brought.
segments.s655-g1202: ز here is the preposition 'from/of' marking material or origin; it links دیبای رومی و چینی (brocade/silk of Rûm and China) to the head noun کمر (belt/girdle). The adjective/nouns precede the head noun in Persian, so 'دیبای رومی و چینی' modifies 'کمر'.
segments.s655-g1203: نثاری is the plural/collective 'offerings' (here functioning as the direct object). که introduces a relative clause ('which was spread'); پوشیده شد is a passive/perfect construction ('was spread/was covered') and روی بوم gives the location ('on/upon the cloth/canvas').
segments.s655-g1204: چنان is an intensifying adverb ('such/so'); باژ here denotes a loud spectacle/clamor (metaphorical 'noise' or striking display). هرگز نیامد is a negated past ('never came/was not seen'), and ز روم specifies the origin/source ('from Rûm'). The clause states that no such spectacle had come from Rûm before.
segments.s655-g1205: This is a compact, slightly inverted poetic phrase: ز دینار پر کرده = 'filled with dinars' (ز marks what fills them), ده چرم گاو = 'ten cowhides' (ده numeral + noun). Together: 'ten cowhides filled with dinars.' The participial phrase پر کرده modifies چرم.
segments.s655-g1206: ساله (part of 'سه ساله' in the full phrase) gives age ('-year-old'), modifying the hides/cattle mentioned earlier. فرستاده شد is a passive past ('was sent'). باژ و ساو are destinations (two place names); و is the coordinating 'and'.
segments.s655-g1207: ز قیصر marks the source ('from Caesar'). یکی نامه‌ای is an indefinite noun phrase ('a certain/one letter'). با نثار means 'with tribute/offerings' (nثار = the accompanying gifts). The preposition با links the letter to its attached tribute.
segments.s655-g1208: نبشته is a written text/participle ('written' or 'the written thing'); سوی marks direction/recipient ('to/toward'); نامور شهریار is the noun phrase 'the renowned sovereign' (نامور = famed, شهریار = king). Together: 'written and directed to the renowned king.'
segments.s655-g537: دل خویش is a possessive phrase: دل "heart/mind" plus the possessive parts (خوی + ش) yielding “one's own heart.” باید که is a modal construction ('it is necessary that / one must') and که introduces the subordinate clause whose verb comes later. درجنگ سخت is an adverbial prepositional phrase ('in fierce/harsh battle') modifying the necessity.
segments.s655-g538: چنان رام دارد is the core of the subordinate clause: چنان 'so', رام 'tame' and دارد here ('has/keeps') expresses holding/keeping that state; the object (the heart) is omitted because it was named earlier. که after دارد introduces the result/extent clause; با + noun ('with/ before') indicates relation or presence of the crown and throne (تاج و تخت). Word order is slightly poetic: adjective/complement before the verb and elided object.
segments.s655-g539: تو را is marked by the object/beneficiary marker را ('to you' / 'for you'). بادا is an optative particle meaning 'may it be' (a prayer/blessing). جهان‌آفرین is the nominative/agent — 'the world‑creator' — and یار ('companion') is the predicate complement: literally 'May the Creator of the world be a friend to you.'
segments.s655-g540: بماناد is an archaic/poetic optative form of ماندن ('may [he] remain/keep'); the subject is still the implied جهان‌آفرین. روشن is a predicative adjective 'bright/clear' that qualifies the following nouns; کلاه و نگین are the coordinated objects ('hat/cap and gem'), meaning 'may he keep the hat and gem bright.'
segments.s655-g541: نهادند is a past plural verb 'they placed/established'. برنامه is the direct object 'a plan'. بر مهر شاه is a prepositional phrase meaning 'for/on the basis of the king's favour' (بر + مهرِ شاه: toward/for the king's affection). The whole clause says they put a plan in motion aimed at the king's favour.
segments.s655-g542: بیاراست is a past tense verb 'he/she/they adorned' (here: 'adorned'). آن خسروی is the subject following the verb (poetic inversion) — 'that royal one / the sovereign.' تاج و گاه are the coordinated direct objects ('crown and seat/occasion'); the verb applies to both. Note the slightly archaic ordering (verb before subject).
segments.s655-g543: برسم کیان is a fixed prepositional phrase meaning 'according to the custom/ceremony of the Kians' (بر + رسم + کیان). خلعت آراستند is a past-tense clause: خلعت 'robe of investiture/robe of honour' as object and آراستند 'they adorned [him with it]'; the direct object (the person adorned) is omitted here but understood from context.
segments.s655-g544: فرستاده را places the messenger as the definite object (را marking it). پیش here means 'in the presence of / before' and اوخواستند is an archaic past construction 'they desired/called him' (i.e., 'they summoned him'). Note the object's fronting (فرستاده را before the verb) and the slightly old-fashioned compound verb form اوخواستند.
segments.s655-g1417: دهم here functions as an ordinal introducer ('tenth') listing a vice or trait. آنک is the archaic relative 'that/which/who' introducing the clause; the phrase از کس ندارد سپاس is literal 'from anyone has no thanks' — a terse classical expression meaning 'does not seek/receive thanks from anyone' (word order: relative + prepositional phrase + verbal predicate).
segments.s655-g1418: به نیکی locates the mental object ('regarding goodness' / 'about doing good'). وهم نیست is a negated copular clause ('has no illusion' / 'is not deluded'), and the subject Yezdān‑shnās (یزدان‌شناس) comes at the end in typical Persian inverted order: 'the God‑knower is not under any illusion about goodness.'
segments.s655-g1419: بدو گفت is 'he said to him' (بودو/بدو = 'to him' + گفت = 'said'). ازین (از + این) = 'from these'; شوم is the adjective 'ill‑omened/bad'; ده here is an imperative/command meaning 'give/name' (classical usage for 'enumerate'), and باگزند qualifies the items as 'harmful' or 'causing harm' — together: 'From these bad ones, give/name ten harmful (items).'
segments.s655-g1420: کدامست is an interrogative 'which/which one is it?'. آهرمن names Ahriman/the devil and زورمند is an adjective 'powerful/mighty'; the clause asks 'which is the mighty Ahriman?' (i.e. which of these is the potent demonic force).
segments.s655-g1421: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed classical phrase 'thus gave an answer' (چنین = 'thus/so', داد = 'gave', پاسخ = 'answer'); به کسری = 'to Khosrow' (دربار title), and که introduces the content clause 'that ...' followed immediately by آز 'greed' as the subject/topic of the reported speech.
segments.s655-g1422: ستمکاره دیوی is a double noun phrase in apposition: 'an oppressive wrong‑doer, a demon' (two epithets describing greed). بود is the past copula 'was' and دیرساز is a predicate noun/epithet ('delayer' — i.e. one who causes delay or procrastination); together they form 'was an oppressive, demonic delayer.'
segments.s655-g1423: که here continues/explains the preceding clause ('so that/that'), او را is the object/topic marker with 'he' (but in this idiom 'one' or 'the greedy man'), and نبینند is a negative subjunctive/optative form 'they/people do not see'. خشنود 'content/pleased' is the predicate; ایچ is an emphatic negation 'at all/none' — together: 'that one is not seen content at all.'
segments.s655-g1424: همه begins 'all' / 'whatever', درفزونیش is a possessive/adverbial nominal meaning 'in his increase/abundance', باشد is the present/subjunctive copula 'is/should be', and بسیچ ('very/much') qualifies the extent — the clause expresses the idea 'however much he has/whatever his abundance, it is (still) very [insufficient]' (classical concision; idiomatically 'it is never enough').
segments.s655-g737: چواین ('چو' + 'این') functions as a temporal conjunction 'when/after this'. کرده باشد is a perfect/subjunctive compound meaning 'had done/was done' (a poetic past reference), followed by the complementizer که and کردیم 'we did/related'; یاد here is a noun 'remembrance/that which was related', so the whole clause means 'when this had been done, as we have recounted'.
segments.s655-g738: سخن را is the direct object 'the speech/account' (را marks definiteness/object). به پیوستگی is an adverbial phrase 'in/with continuity' (به + noun), and داد is the past verb 'gave' (repeated here for rhetorical emphasis or metrical reasons). Thus: 'he gave the speech in continuous form.'
segments.s655-g739: فرستادگان is the plural subject 'the envoys'. خواندند is the simple past plural 'they read/sang/recited'. آفرین is an interjection 'Bravo!' or an exclamation of praise reported by the verb.
segments.s655-g740: که introduces a content clause ('that'). از here expresses cause or relation 'because of / with respect to' (they say the Khaghan is joyful concerning the king). شاه is 'the king', شادست is a predicative adjective + copula 'is joyful', and خاقان چین means 'the Khaghan of China' (خاقان = khaghan, چین = China).
segments.s655-g741: که begins a relative/explanatory clause 'for/that'. در پرده پوشیده is a prepositional phrase 'hidden in/behind a veil' (پوشیده is the past participle 'hidden, covered' modifying the preceding noun). رویان اوی is a possessive phrase: رویان is a poetic/archaic form referring to faces/appearance and اوی is the third‑person possessive 'his/her', so together 'her face(s) was/were hidden.'
segments.s655-g742: ز دیدار آنکس is an ablative/causal circumstantial 'at the sight of that person' (ز = 'from/at (poetic)'). نپوشند is the negative present/subjunctive/past-in-narrative plural form meaning 'they did not cover/conceal' (here 'did not avert/turn away'), and روی is 'face' — so 'they did not hide their faces at seeing that person.'
segments.s655-g743: شهنشاه is the subject 'the emperor'. بشنید is the past tense 'he heard'. ز ایشان سخن literally 'speech from them' (ز = 'from', ایشان = 'them', سخن = 'words/speech') — 'the emperor heard words from them.'
segments.s655-g744: برو is a poetic/discourse particle meaning 'thereupon/with that' (not the literal imperative 'go' here); تازه شد is 'became fresh/renewed' (شد = 'became'), and روزگار کهن is 'the old/ancient times' — together: 'thereupon the old age was renewed.'
segments.s655-g633: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; بنده is 'servant' (direct object/source). نخواهد is the third-person present/future negative of خواستن ('will not want/ask'); جز ... از راستی = 'nothing except/from honesty' (جز follows the negation to mean 'except' or 'only'). Together this clause says 'he will ask of a servant nothing but honesty.'
segments.s655-g634: نجوید is the present form 'seek' (here interpreted as part of the negated verbal idea introduced by نخواهد: 'will not seek'). به is instrumental/means 'by/with'; داد means 'justice' (not the verb 'gave'); اندرون کاستی = 'inner deficiency/shortcoming' (an object phrase). The clause reads 'he will not seek, by justice, the inner deficiency.'
segments.s655-g635: ازو باد is a fixed poetic optative: ازو 'from him' + باد (optative particle) = 'may there be from him.' برشاه ایران درود = 'a blessing/greeting upon the king of Iran' (بر = 'upon', شاه = 'king'); word order is typical poetic preposition + noun + object.
segments.s655-g636: خداوند here names the granter ('the Lord' / 'God'); the verb of giving is omitted (elliptical) as is common in Persian poetry, so the following nouns are gifts. شمشیر و کوپال و خود list those gifts: 'sword, Kūpāl (a weapon/name), and himself/his own presence' — خود functions as 'his own / himself' (protective presence).
segments.s655-g637: خداوند again is the granter ('God' / 'the Lord'); دانایی و تاج و تخت are a triumvirate of gifts ('wisdom, crown, and throne'). Note the poetic attachment of و ('and') to the nouns (e.g. وتاج) for meter and flow.
segments.s655-g638: ز is the poetic 'from'; پیروزگر is 'the victor/conqueror' as source or agent; یافته is a past participle/verb 'has found/obtained' placed at the end (typical Persian verb position); کام و بخت are the objects 'desire/wish and fortune' that have been obtained. The whole phrase means 'he has obtained desire and fortune from the victor.'
segments.s655-g639: بداند is the third-person present/subjunctive 'let him know / may he know / he knows' (here optative/imperative sense fits the blessing/formulaic tone). جهاندار خسرونژاد is a titled subject in apposition: 'the world-ruler, of the race of Khosrow' (title + lineage).
segments.s655-g640: خردمند is the predicate/complement 'wise (man)'; با introduces accompanying qualities 'with' (با + list). سنگ here means 'gem/precious stone' (not generic rock), فرهنگ means 'learning/culture', and راد is an adjective 'generous/noble'. Together they enumerate the wise man's attributes: 'with gems, learning, and generosity.'
segments.s655-g425: همه (all) precedes the noun مرزبانان (frontier-guards) to quantify it. زرین کمر is an attributive phrase literally ‘golden waist/belt’ (زرین modifying کمر) describing those guards; in poetry this reads as a compound ‘golden‑belted’ with no copula.
segments.s655-g426: بلوچی و گیلی is a coordinated ethnic label (‘Baloch and Gilaki’) modifying the same guards. به + noun (به زرین سپر) here functions prepositionally to indicate accompaniment/instrumental sense ‘with’ (poetic usage), so به زرین سپر = ‘with golden shields.’
segments.s655-g427: سراسر is an adverb ‘throughout/entirely’ placed before the locative phrase. بدان is the fused locative (به + آن = ‘to/at that’), so بدان بارگاه = ‘to/at that palace’; آمدند is the past‑plural verb ‘they came’ placed at the end in normal Persian word order.
segments.s655-g428: پرستنده is a noun ‘worshipper/devotee’; نزدیک شاه is a prepositional/locative phrase ‘near the king’ (نزدیک used adverbially before the noun). آمدند is again past plural ‘they came’ — the verb finishes the clause.
segments.s655-g429: چوسیصدز is a compressed poetic sequence: چو (as/like) + سیصد (three hundred) + ز (of) → ‘as if three hundred of…’. پیلان is the plural oblique ‘of elephants’ governed by ز; زرین qualifies the following noun phrase as ‘golden’. ستام is a rare/poetic form here and does not match the supplied senses precisely (see note); the whole phrase functions as a descriptive image (‘as if three hundred golden elephants…’).
segments.s655-g430: ببردند is a past‑plural form meaning ‘they carried/took’ (the prefixed ب is a classical/poetic verbal prefix common in Ferdowsi). و is the conjunction ‘and’; شمشیر زرین نیام strings together weapon and its accoutrement: شمشیر زرین = ‘golden sword’ or more idiomatically here ‘swords with golden scabbards’, نیام = ‘scabbard’. The verb (ببردند) governs the whole latter phrase.
segments.s655-g431: درخشیدن is a verbal noun/infinitive ‘the shining/gleam’ serving as the subject; it is followed by a coordinated list تیغ و ژوپین و خشت (sword, gambeson/padded jacket, and brick) — note the poetic attachment of و to the third item وخشت. The clause lists items that shine.
segments.s655-g432: تو گویی is an idiomatic, somewhat archaic expression meaning ‘one would say/it seems to you’ (here equivalent to English ‘you would think’). که introduces the content clause; زر اندر آهن سرشت is inverted poetic word order meaning ‘that gold was fashioned into iron’ (اندر = in/into, سرشت is a past/participial poetic form of ‘to fashion/create’ used here in the sense ‘was wrought’).
segments.s655-g729: چنان is an adverb 'thus/so'; باید is a modal/impersonal present 'must/should'; اکنون is a time adverb 'now'. که introduces a subordinate clause whose subject follows: خاقان چین = 'the Khagan of China' (خاقان 'khagan' + چین 'China' as its specifier). The whole segment sets up a clause: 'It must now be so that the Khagan of China...'.
segments.s655-g730: This continues the subordinate clause: دل = 'heart' (the thing affected); ماکند here is an older/compounded form (treated here as part of the verb complex) conveying 'make (our)'; شاد is the predicative adjective 'glad/happy'. بر به گزین is a verbal phrase with گزین 'choose' (jussive/subjunctive sense), while the prepositions بر ... به mark the directional/relational sense ('choose as / for / over'). Altogether: '...who will make our hearts glad, choose (him).'
segments.s655-g731: کسی را (کسی + object marker را) is the definite/direct-object phrase 'someone (as the one to be sent)'. فرستم is first-person singular present/jussive 'I will send / let me send'. که begins a relative clause 'who ...'; دارد خرد = 'possesses wisdom' with دارد as the present-tense verb and خرد as the noun 'wisdom'.
segments.s655-g732: شبستان او = 'his bedchamber' (noun + postposed possessive او, a common poetic order). سر به سر is an adverbial idiom meaning 'completely/throughout' (literally 'head to head') and modifies بنگرد, which is the third-person subjunctive/jussive of نگریستن 'to look/inspect' ('let him look' / 'he should look'). The clause thus orders that the sent man inspect the chamber thoroughly.
segments.s655-g733: یکی برگزیند is a jussive/optative clause 'let him choose one' (یکی = one/a single person; برگزیند = choose, subjunctive). که introduces the relative clause 'who ...'; نامی ترست = 'is more famous' with نامی 'famous' and ترست as a comparative predicate 'is more'.
segments.s655-g734: به خاقان چین marks the perspective or relation 'in the eyes of / to the Khagan of China' (به as a dative/relational marker; خاقان چین = 'Khagan of China'). برگرامی ترست is a comparative predicate 'is more honored/esteemed' (برگرامی 'honored', ترست 'is more'), so the whole segment qualifies the chosen one as especially esteemed by that ruler.
segments.s655-g735: ببیند is third-person subjunctive/jussive 'let him see' or 'he should see'. که introduces an indirect question; تا چون here functions together as an interrogative phrase 'what sort / how' (old/poetic ordering), followed by بود 'was' (past copula) and مادرش 'his mother' (مادر + possessive ش). The clause asks him to ascertain what kind of woman his mother had been.
segments.s655-g736: بود is the past copula 'was'; از نژاد کیان = a prepositional noun phrase 'of the lineage of the Kians' (از = 'of/from', نژاد = 'lineage/race', کیان = 'the Kian kings/dynasty'). گوهرش is a possessive noun 'her gem/offspring' (literal 'her jewel') and here refers to her child/line, tying the mother's quality to the noble descent being inspected.
segments.s655-g1681: چنین گفت is a fixed formula ('thus he said') withAncillary adverb چنين before the past-tense verb گفت. کان کو بود دادگر is an archaic relative clause: کان کو = 'the one who' (older form of کسی که), بود is the copula 'is/was', and دادگر is the predicate 'just / a just ruler'. The clause literally: 'the one who was/is just.'
segments.s655-g1682: خرد دارد is a simple nominal predicate: خرد 'wisdom' + دارد (present/persistent possession) 'has/possesses'. The subsequent coordination با و links a series of qualities: رای 'counsel/opinion', شرم 'modesty', هنر 'excellence/virtue'. The verb دارد governs the whole coordinated list.
segments.s655-g1683: بپرسیدش is an imperative form 'ask' with the enclitic object pronoun -ش 'him' attached (beporsid + -ash). از دوستان کهن is a prepositional phrase 'of/from the old friends' where کهن follows دوستان as a postpositive adjective 'old/seasoned'.
segments.s655-g1684: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. باشند is the third-person plural subjunctive/present (classical style) meaning 'are'/'be' here used to state a general quality. هم modifies the following noun (here 'together/also' → 'steadfast/aligned'), کوشه is an old/ambiguous word (contextually 'steadfast/constant' or 'of one quarter'); و links to the final attribute یک‌سخن 'of one speech/one mind'.
segments.s655-g1685: چنین داد پاسخ is another word-order variant meaning 'thus gave an answer' (چنین adverb + داد 'gave' + پاسخ 'answer'). که heads the reported content 'that'. از is the preposition 'from/among', مرد دوست here denotes 'a man who is a friend/faithful man' (a qualifying noun phrase).
segments.s655-g1686: جوانمردی is a noun meaning 'chivalry, gallantry'. داد دادن is a light verbal-nominal construction 'to give justice' or more idiomatically 'to administer justice' (داد as noun 'justice' + دادن 'to give'). نکوست is an archaic predicate meaning 'is good/fitting'. The whole says these qualities are good in a true man.
segments.s655-g1687: نخواهد is a negative modal 'will not' (classical usage can also imply 'there will be no one who...'). به تو is the indirect object 'to you'. بد functions as 'bad/ill' (here 'harm'). به آزرم کس is a short phrase where آزرم is ambiguous/archaic (possibly a proper name or 'honour'); کس is 'anyone/someone' — contextually it expresses that no one will do you ill (out of respect/honour or directed at someone).
segments.s655-g1688: به سختی is a locative phrase 'in hardship' (به + سختی). بود is the copula 'was/is/will be' used here as a general statement of existence. یار 'companion/ally' and فریادرس 'rescuer/succour' are coordinated predicates: '(there) will be a companion and a rescuer in hardship.'
segments.s655-g1177: This is a pair of parallel prepositional phrases introduced by the poetic preposition ز (ز … و ز …) meaning “from.” Each ز governs a following noun phrase: ز کوه بلوج = “from (the) Baluch mountain” and ز دشت سروچ = “from (the) plain of Sarvach.” The order is preposition + head noun + optional placename, typical of older/poetic Persian.
segments.s655-g1178: گرازان is the subject (a collective/plural noun: literally “boars,” here understood as a band). برفتند is a past-tense 3rd-person-plural verb (“they went/departed”). گردان کوچ is a postverbal noun phrase in apposition or description — “a troop (گردان) on migration (کوچ)” — placed after the verb for poetic rhythm. The clause reads as Subject – Verb – descriptive noun phrase.
segments.s655-g1179: همه پاک here functions as a fronted adverbial/subject marker meaning “all of them / entirely.” پاک is emphatic/adverbial (“entirely”/“altogether”) modifying the following action. با هدیه و با نثار are two coordinated prepositional phrases (repeated با for parallelism) meaning “with gifts and with offerings/tributes.”
segments.s655-g1180: به پیش is a directional phrase “to/in the presence of.” سراپرده is a compound noun meaning the inner chamber/curtained apartment, and شهریار follows it as a possessive/genitive apposition: “the king’s inner tent/inner chamber.” The whole phrase marks direction/target of the action in the previous segment.
segments.s655-g1181: بدان is an archaic locative/composite meaning “to/in that” (ب + دان); it introduces location/target. شهرشد is a poetic compound here functioning as a verb phrase meaning the arrival/establishment in the city (verb before subject, a common poetic inversion). شهریار بزرگ is the nominative subject (postverbal because of inversion): “the great king.”
segments.s655-g1182: که introduces a relative clause modifying شهریار بزرگ: “who …”. ازمیش here is the fused prepositional phrase (literally from + sheep/ewe in the underlying text) indicating the object/source from which the wolf must withdraw; the exact lexical segmentation is archaic/poetic. کوته کند is a present/future-purposive sense of کند with an adjective کوته meaning “to shorten/withdraw” (literally “makes short/withdraw”). چنگ گرگ is a genitive construction “the wolf’s claw.” Taken together: “who makes the wolf withdraw its claw from the ewe.”
segments.s655-g1183: به فر is an agentive/instrumental phrase here: به + فر (فر = royal/divine glory) meaning “by the glory.” جهاندار کسری is a title in apposition: جهاندار = “world-holder/sovereign” and کسری = Kisra/Khosrow (proper name/title). سپهر here is the domain/sky/heaven often used poetically for the realm. The whole phrase names the agent whose glory effects the change.
segments.s655-g1184: دگرگونه‌تر شد is a comparative + past of شدن: “became different / was changed (more different).” The phrase به کین و به مهر uses the preposition به twice in parallel to mark two contrasting modes/causes: کین = “hatred/vengeance” and مهر = “affection/favor.” Grammatically, the clause states the result (شد) and then cites the contrasting motives or tones (in enmity and in love).
segments.s655-g249: از ایرانیان is an ablative/prepositional phrase ('from/on the Iranians') marking the target of the action. باز خواهند کین is a poetic clause: خواهند is the 3rd-person plural future auxiliary ('they will'), کین is the noun 'vengeance' used as the verbal idea (the verb 'to take' is omitted in poetic economy), and باز is an adverb ('again' / emphatic). Word order is inverted/condensed for meter, so the phrase means 'they will take vengeance on the Iranians.'
segments.s655-g250: نماند is the verb 'نَمانَد' (= will not remain / be left), with the negative prefix n- fused to مانَد; here it expresses that nothing will be left. بروبوم ایران‌زمین is a locative phrase ('in/throughout the land of Iran'), بروبوم being an older/poetic word for 'homeland/land' and ایران‌زمین 'the Iranian realm'. Together: 'no one/none will remain in the land of Iran.'
segments.s655-g251: نه کس is the negative pronoun 'no one'; پای بر خاک ایران نهاد is the fixed verbal phrase 'پای بر خاک نهاد' (پا نهادن = 'to set foot'), where نهاد is the past of نهادن ('put/placed') used idiomatically for 'set (one's) foot on'. Word order is straightforward: 'No one set foot on the soil of Iran.'
segments.s655-g252: نه is the sentential negation ('nor/not'); زین here functions as an archaic/genitive-like 'ز این' ('of/from this') introducing the following noun phrase. پادشاهی 'kingship/kingdom' is the noun, and the sequence ببد کرد یاد is an older poetic periphrasis: ببد (archaic form of بود/ 'was') plus کرد ('made/did') with یاد ('remembrance') expresses that no remembrance/mention was made — i.e., 'that kingship was not remembered.' The order and auxiliary verbs are archaic and compressed for meter.
segments.s655-g253: اگر is the conditional 'if'. شاه را رای is an older/genitive-like construction where را marks the definite possessor ('the king's counsel/plan'). رای means 'counsel/plan', and کینست is a fused classical copula (کین است = 'is vengeance'); وجنگ simply adds 'and war'. So: 'If the king's counsel is vengeance and war...'.
segments.s655-g254: ازو 'from/by him' marks the agent/source (the king). رام گردد is a verbal phrase meaning 'becomes tame / is tamed' (رام = tame; گردد an archaic/poetic form of 'گردد' = 'becomes/is made'). به دریا نهنگ is a poetically inverted nominative/accusative phrase meaning 'the whale of the sea' (normal prose would be نهنگِ دریا); the inversion serves the meter. Altogether: 'a whale of the sea is tamed by him.'
segments.s655-g255: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is the past tense 'he heard'. ز ایرانیان is a prepositional phrase 'from the Iranians'. شهریار (the king) appears after the clause because of poetic inversion (normal prose order would put the subject before the verb). So: 'When the king heard from the Iranians...'.
segments.s655-g256: This is a triadic list introduced by the preposition ز ('of/from/about') and linked by an archaic fused conjunction-form وز (a poetic combination of و + ز) before the second and third items. بزم, پرخاش and کارزار are the three nouns being reported: 'the feast/banquet', 'the reproach/attack', and 'the battlefield/battle'. In short: 'about the feast, about the reproach, and about the battle.'
segments.s655-g345: گماند is an impersonal / past idea ('it seemed' / 'one thought'); it introduces the subordinate clause that follows. کزو is a fused relative/compound meaning 'from him' ('that one might pass from him'), followed by the verb بگذری in the subjunctive/infinitival sense 'to pass by/get past'. راه نیست is the predicate 'there is no way' (راه as 'way/method'), so the clause literally reads 'it seemed that from him to pass there is no way.'
segments.s655-g346: و‌گر is a conjunctive/conditional particle ('and if' / 'and indeed'), here introducing a complementary thought. در زمانه 'in the age' is a locative phrase; جز او شاه نیست is a negative existential ('there is no king except him'). Word order is typical classical Persian: locative first, then exception particle (جز), then the subject (او) and predicate (شاه نیست).
segments.s655-g347: بیاگاهد is a past/perfect-form verb 'he/it came/appeared' (archaic classical form) and precedes the temporal adverb اکنون 'now'. چومن is a preposed modifier 'like me' and modifies جنگجوی 'warrior', so the phrase means 'now a warrior like me has appeared.'
segments.s655-g348: شوم is first‑person subjunctive/optative 'I shall be / I will become' expressing the speaker's resolution or intention; it takes the rest of the phrase as a circumstantial complement. با + سواران چین 'with the Chinese horsemen' is an instrumental/associative phrase (چین = 'China' / 'the Chinese'), and پیش اوی 'before him / in his presence' uses پیش plus the enclitic pronoun اوی ('him'). Word order places the subjunctive verb before the accompanying prepositional phrases.
segments.s655-g349: خردمند مردی is an indefinite noun phrase 'a wise man' (adjective before noun). به indicates the indirect object 'to' (به خاقان چین = 'to the Khaghan of China'), with خاقان چین as the title plus the place/people marker.
segments.s655-g350: چنین گفت is 'thus he said' (چنین 'thus' + گفت 'said'); it introduces direct speech. کای is a vocative particle 'O' (addressing), followed by شهریار زمین 'sovereign/king of the earth' — a grand vocative addressing the ruler. (Note: the word گفت is the past 'said' though the provided sense list is general.)
segments.s655-g351: This clause begins with the negative imperative مکن 'do not' addressed to تو 'you'. تو با شاه ایران مکن رزم literally 'you, with the King of Iran do not (engage in) battle' — رزم is the object/idea 'war, battle' and یاد (here) begins the negative instruction continued in the next segment ('یاد مده'). Word order places the negative particle before the verb/phrase.
segments.s655-g352: مده is another negative imperative 'do not give' (classical form). پادشاهی و لشکر are the coordinated objects 'the kingship and the army'. به باد is a prepositional idiom 'to the wind' (= to ruin/for nothing); باد is literal 'wind' used idiomatically. The whole clause warns 'do not give the kingdom and the army to the wind.'
segments.s655-g273: This clause says who/what will be acted upon and the speaker's first action. جهان (the world) is the theme; از + بدان (= ‘of/from the wicked’) marks the source to be cleared. پاک is used adjectivally/adverbially (‘clean/utterly’) and بی‌خوکنم is a first-person present/jussive verb phrase ‘I make sleepless / I keep awake’, here an active verb describing an aggressive act toward the wicked. Word order is Theme (جهان) → source-marker (از + آنان/بدان) → qualifier (پاک) → verb (بی‌خوکنم).
segments.s655-g274: This clause gives a second, coordinated action. بداد و دهش is a paired phrase invoking ‘justice (داد) and giving/bounty (دهش)’ (an idiomatic pairing meaning rule by justice and generosity), linked by the conjunction و; کشوری نو کنم is a verb phrase with کنم (first-person present/jussive) that takes کشوری نو (‘a new realm/realm new’) as its object/complement: ‘I will make a new realm.’ The final کنم is the speaker’s promise/future action.
segments.s655-g275: همه نامداران is the subject phrase ‘all the renowned / all the famed ones.’ فروماندند is a past tense, third-person-plural verb meaning here they were left behind/put to shame or failed to keep up; the verb is passive/intransitive in sense — ‘they were overcome/humbled.’
segments.s655-g276: به پوزش برو is an imperative/infinitival idea ‘go to (ask) pardon’ where به marks the goal/purpose (to/for pardon) and برو is an imperative or hortative ‘go.’ آفرین خواندند follows: آفرین is an interjection/acclamation (‘bravo’), and خواندند is simple past, 3pl (‘they uttered/called/sang’), so the phrase means ‘they went to beg pardon and exclaimed “afrin”.’
segments.s655-g277: که‌ای is a poetic/vocative particle meaning ‘O’ or introducing an address (literally ‘that—O’); شاه پیروز is the vocative ‘O victorious king.’ با فر و داد is a prepositional phrase ‘with glory and justice’ (با + noun + و + noun) modifying the felicitation that follows.
segments.s655-g278: زمانه is the subject ‘the age/epoch’ (or personified fortune). به دیدار = ‘at/for the sight/coming’ (به marks relation/purpose), توشاد is an older optative/jussive form meaning ‘be glad at you’ (to + شاد = ‘be glad’ addressed to the king), and باد is the optative particle ‘may’ (fixed expression: توشاد باد = ‘may it be joyful [at your coming]’). Together they form a wish: ‘May the age, at your sight, be glad.’
segments.s655-g279: همه نامداران is the topical subject ‘all the renowned (men).’ تو را is the object/possessive phrasing ‘to you / your (service)’ with را as the direct-object/emphatic marker. بنده‌ایم is first-person-plural present ‘we are servants’ (a humble, formulaic self-description meaning ‘we are your servants’). The line thus expresses collective humility and allegiance.
segments.s655-g280: به فرمان و رایت is a prepositional phrase ‘by (your) command and standard/banner’ (به = by/at, فرمان = command, رایت = banner/standard). سرافگنده‌ایم is first-person-plural ‘we are ashamed / abashed / humbled’ (a stative verb); the clause gives the reason or posture (‘we are humbled before your command and banner’).
segments.s655-g313: بسغد اندرون بود خاقان is a location‑first clause: بسغد (Sogd/Sogdiana) + اندرون (inside) and then the past copula بود with the subject خاقان (the Khaghan). The conjunction/relativizer که introduces the following clause that continues with شاه (the shah) as subject of the next verb.
segments.s655-g314: به گرگان همی رای زد با سپاه: به گرگان is a directional prepositional phrase ('toward/to Gorgan'); همی is an archaic/progressive marker indicating ongoing or habitual action; رای زد is an idiomatic verbal combination (رای = plan/counsel, زد from زدن used idiomatically 'to take/make a decision/plan'); با سپاه is an accompaniment/instrumental phrase 'with an army.' Word order places the direction before the verb phrase.
segments.s655-g315: ز خویشان ارجاسب و افراسیاب uses ز (poetic 'from' / 'out of') to mark origin/association: 'from the relatives (or among the kin) of Arjasb and Afrasiab.' The two names are coordinated by و and stand in apposition to خویشان.
segments.s655-g316: شده سغد یکسر چو دریای آب: شده (become) precedes the subject سغد (inverted order: 'Sogd has become'); یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/wholly'; چو introduces a simile 'like/as'; دریای آب is a genitive/construct phrase 'a sea of water.' Together: 'Sogd has become wholly like a sea of water.'
segments.s655-g317: همی‌گفت خاقان سپاه مرا: همی‌گفت is the habitual/imperfective 'kept saying'; خاقان is the speaker; سپاه followed by the clitic pieces (م ... را) marks 'my army' with emphasis/definiteness so this phrase begins the khaghan's reported words. The clause is left incomplete here because it continues in the next segment.
segments.s655-g318: زمین برنتابد کلاه مرا: زمین 'the earth' is the subject; برنتابد is a poetic/compound negative jussive (بر‑ + نتابد) meaning roughly 'may not bear/hold' or 'shall not endure'; کلاه مرا ('my hat/crown' with را marking definiteness) is the second object in parallel: the speaker invokes that the earth should not (figuratively) take or hold his army nor his crown. This is an archaic, elliptical oath‑style construction.
segments.s655-g319: از ایدر سپه سوی ایران کشیم: از ایدر 'from here' (ایدر = poetic 'here'); سپه 'the army' is the object/topic; سوی ایران 'toward Iran' marks direction; کشیم is a first‑person‑plural jussive/optative of کشیدن used here in the sense 'let us lead/march (the army).' The verbal ending -یم encodes 'we', so the subject is implied.
segments.s655-g320: وز ایران به دشت دلیران کشیم: وز is a poetic / conjunctive form (here 'and/from') linking the second clause to the first; ایران is the source or point of reference; به دشت دلیران 'to the plain of the brave' is the destination phrase; کشیم repeats the same first‑person‑plural 'let us bring/lead/march.' The clause is parallel in structure to the previous one and uses the plural verbal ending to include the speaker(s).
segments.s655-g289: بدیدند is a past‑tense, 3rd‑person plural verb 'they saw/bewhheld'. The prepositional phrase بر چهره شاه ماه follows the verb here (poetic inversion) and literally means 'on the face of the moon‑like king' — چهره is the head/face, شاه is the possessor 'king', and ماه is a descriptive noun likening him to the moon (epithet after the noun).
segments.s655-g290: خروشی is an indefinite noun 'a roar/clamour' functioning as the subject; برآمد is 3rd‑person singular past 'arose/issued forth'. The poetic preposition ز (here 'from') plus درگاه شاه ('the king's court/threshold') marks the source of the sound: 'a roar rose from the king's court.'
segments.s655-g291: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. برزد is an archaic past verb meaning 'rose/appeared' (here 'lifted/put forth'), with سر ('head' or 'top') as the thing that rises; از کوه رخشان چراغ is a compressed modifier 'the radiant lamp from the mountain' (poetic order places modifiers after the noun), so the whole clause reads 'when the radiant lamp lifted its head from the mountain.'
segments.s655-g292: زمین شد is a simple past copular construction 'the earth became'. به indicates likeness or manner here ('in/into the likeness of'), followed by کردار زرین جناغ: کردار used as 'form/appearance' and زرین جناغ 'golden sternum/breastbone' — so 'the earth became in the form of a golden breastbone.'
segments.s655-g293: This is a compound sequence: خروش آمد is 'a roar came/rose' (simple past, 3rd person). The conjunction و 'and' links the next noun phrase ناله گاو دم, where ناله ('moan/cry') is followed by the possessor گاو ('of the cow') and the modifier دم ('breath/moment'), giving 'the cow's moan at (its) breath/last gasp.'
segments.s655-g294: ببستند is past, 3rd‑person plural 'they tied/bound' (transitive). The prepositional phrase بر پیل رویینه خم follows and marks the location/target: بر 'on/upon' پیل 'elephant' modified by رویینه 'armored/iron‑clad' and خم 'curve/hump' — 'they bound (it) on the elephant's armored hump.' The object (what was bound) is implicit and connected to the preceding noun phrase (the cow's moan/breath).
segments.s655-g295: دمادم is an adverb 'again and again/constantly' placed at the start. The core clause is سپاه آمد 'a host/army came' (past 3rd person) with the prepositional phrase به لشکر گه 'to the army's place/camp' inserted (گه here denotes a place/time). Poetic inversion places the adverb and locative element before the verb.
segments.s655-g296: تبیره زنان is an agentive phrase: زنان is here best read as a participial/agentive 'those striking/strikers' rather than 'women', so 'تبیره‑zanan' are the tabire‑strikers (a musical/warband element). برگرفتند راه is a fixed verbal phrase 'they took up the road' (past 3rd plural), meaning 'they set out / took to the road.'
segments.s655-g297: بدرگاه شد is a compact clause meaning 'came into the (kingly) presence / entered the court': بدرگاه ('to the court / presence at court') is a locative/goal, followed by شد (past tense) as the intransitive verb 'came/entered'. یزدگرد دبیر is an appositive noun phrase 'Yazdegerd, the scribe/secretary' (name + title) identifying the subject who entered.
segments.s655-g298: This phrase lists officials/names in apposition to the subject: ابا رای‌زن is a personal/name-unit (here functioning as a name/title), followed by موبد ('mōbed', a Zoroastrian priest) and اردشیر (the proper name Ardashir). The sequence is nominative apposition to the preceding subject; word order (name/title after the verb) is typical of Persian narrative enumeration.
segments.s655-g299: نبشتند نامه is a past-tense verb phrase 'they wrote a letter(s)' (نبشتند = they wrote), with نامه as direct object. به هر کشوری is a prepositional phrase 'to each/every country/realm' (به = to/for, هر = each/every, کشوری = 'of the country/realm'), indicating the recipients.
segments.s655-g300: بهر نامداری و هرمهتری is a purpose/benefit phrase modifying the letters: بهر ('for' / 'for the purpose of') governs two coordinated nouns: نامداری ('renown, fame') and هرمهتری ('every great one / any distinguished person'). The conjunction و links the two goals/recipients of the action.
segments.s655-g301: که آغاز a subordinating particle introducing reported content 'that/which': که شد شاه با لشکر از بهر رزم = 'that the king went/was with an army for the sake of battle.' شد is past (arrived/went/occurred) and functions as the main past verb; با لشکر is 'with an army'; از بهر رزم = 'for the sake of battle' (از here marks reason/purpose).
segments.s655-g302: شما ... را مسازید بزم is an address to 'you' (شما) with a negative-prescriptive verb form مسازید here meaning 'do not make / do not seat' (a prohibitive sense in context). کهتری را (definite object marked by را) = 'the lesser/one of lower rank' is the object; بزم ('banquet/feast/assembly') indicates the role or place being forbidden. The whole clause is an imperative injunction: 'you shall not set the inferior one at the feast.'
segments.s655-g303: بفرمود نامه is a formal/perhaps honorific past verb 'he commanded/sent (a) letter' (بفرمود = he issued/said/commanded), with نامه as its object. بخاقان چین functions as the indirect object/destination: بخاقان here refers to the Khaghan (ruler) — a proper title not represented by the given dictionary senses — and چین is 'China', forming 'to the Khaghan of China.'
segments.s655-g304: فغانیش را هم بکرد is built from فغانیش ('his/that lamentation') as object + را هم ('ra' object marker + هم = 'also'), with بکرد (past 'he did/caused') as the verb: 'he also made/performed his lament.' آفرین here functions as an exclamatory noun 'praise/acclamation' and is the second noun coordinated by the verb, so the clause means 'he made lamentation and (gave) acclamation/praise.'

sentences.s655-s175: A wise man said to the Khaghan of China, thus: 'O sovereign of the earth...'
sentences.s655-s176: Do not wage war against the King of Iran; do not hand the kingship and the army over to the wind (to ruin).
sentences.s655-s137: I will cleanse the world of the wicked and, by justice and bounty, make a new realm.
sentences.s655-s138: All the famed ones were put to shame; they went to beg pardon and cried 'Bravo.'
sentences.s655-s139: O victorious king, may the age, with glory and justice, be glad at your coming.
sentences.s655-s140: All the famed are your servants; by your command and banner we are ashamed (humbled).
sentences.s655-s157: The Khaghan was inside Sogd, and the shah resolved to move against Gorgan with an army.
sentences.s655-s158: From the kin of Arjasb and Afrasiab, Sogd became entirely like a sea of water.
sentences.s655-s159: The Khaghan kept saying, 'May the earth not hold my army, nor take my crown.'
sentences.s655-s160: From here let us lead the army toward Iran, and from Iran let us bring it to the plain of the brave.
sentences.s655-s145: They beheld upon the moon‑faced king's visage; a roar arose from the king's court.
sentences.s655-s146: When the radiant lamp lifted its head from the mountain, the earth became like a golden breastbone.
sentences.s655-s147: A roar came, and the cow's moan at (its) breath — they bound it to the elephant's iron‑clad curve.
sentences.s655-s148: Again and again a host arrived at the army's camp; the tabire‑strikers took to the road.
sentences.s655-s149: Yazdegerd entered the royal presence, (along with) the scribe Aba Rayzan and the mobed Ardashir.
sentences.s655-s150: They wrote letters to every country, for the sake of renown and every great one.
sentences.s655-s151: That the king went forth with an army for battle — you must not make the lesser one (a place) at the feast.
sentences.s655-s152: He sent a letter to the Khaghan of China; he also made lamentation and uttered praise.
